reasoning: Confirmed real. The sink at functions.php lines 1074-1075 echoes `design_custom_css` inside a `<style>` block using `esc_html()`. That escaper encodes `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, and `'` as HTML entities, but CSS injection payloads such as `} body { background-image: url(//attacker.com) }` contain none of those characters and pass through completely unmodified. The `sanitize_settings()` callback in setup.php (lines 277-396) applies no sanitization to `design_custom_css` — there is no `wp_kses()`, `sanitize_textarea_field()`, or equivalent filter for this key. The value is stored raw via `register_setting()`. The hook `add_action('login_head', ...)` fires on the WordPress login page for all visitors, so injected CSS is rendered to every user hitting the login page. Auth is correctly identified as administrator-only (`manage_options` capability via WordPress settings API). Correct escaper would be `wp_strip_all_tags()` or a CSS-specific allowlist. The wrong-context escaper (`esc_html` inside `<style>`) is the confirmed vulnerability. No additional guards or sanitizers were missed.
